learning_objectives: Understand the von Neumann architecture and its components
Identify the roles of CPU, memory, and I/O in a computer system
Convert between binary, decimal, and hexadecimal number systems
readings: Patterson & Hennessy, Computer Organization and Design, Ch. 1
Nisan & Schocken, The Elements of Computing Systems, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: This course bridges the gap between software and hardware. Understanding how a computer actually executes your code makes you a better programmer, enabling you to write faster software and debug mysterious performance issues.
key_concepts: Von Neumann architecture: stored program concept
CPU components: ALU, control unit, registers
Memory hierarchy overview: registers, cache, RAM, disk
Binary and hexadecimal number systems
Twos complement representation for signed integers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Use a CPU simulator to trace the fetch-decode-execute cycle for simple instructions, observing register and memory state changes at each step
setup_requirements: CPU simulator software installed (MARS MIPS or similar)

review.value: Instructions and data share a single memory bus, meaning the CPU often waits for data; this bottleneck drives the need for caches and out-of-order execution
Negative numbers are represented by flipping all bits and adding 1; this allows the same hardware adder to handle both addition and subtraction

class_schedule.topic: Assembly Language and the Instruction Set Architecture
learning_objectives: Write simple programs in MIPS assembly language
Understand how high-level constructs map to assembly instructions
Explain the role of the instruction set architecture as a hardware/software interface
readings: Patterson & Hennessy, Ch. 2 - Instructions: Language of the Computer

lecture_notes.introduction: Assembly language reveals what the CPU actually does. Every if-statement, loop, and function call in Python or C compiles down to sequences of these simple instructions. Understanding assembly makes you a far better debugger and optimizer.
key_concepts: MIPS instruction format: R-type, I-type, J-type
Arithmetic instructions: add, sub, mul, div
Memory instructions: lw (load word), sw (store word)
Branch and jump instructions for control flow
Translating C/Python constructs to assembly
lecture_notes.demonstration: Translate a simple C function (iterative factorial) to MIPS assembly step by step, then run it in the MARS simulator

review.value: The instruction set architecture is the contract between hardware and software: hardware promises to implement these instructions, and compilers promise to generate only these instructions
MIPS is RISC (few simple instructions, fixed length); x86 is CISC (many complex instructions, variable length). Modern CISC CPUs internally decode to RISC-like micro-ops

learning_objectives: Trace instruction execution through the CPU datapath
Understand how control signals direct data through the processor
Explain the single-cycle processor design and its limitations

lecture_notes.introduction: The datapath is the highway system inside the CPU: data flows from registers through the ALU and back, directed by control signals. Understanding this reveals why some instructions are faster than others and why pipelining is necessary.
key_concepts: Datapath components: register file, ALU, multiplexers, memory units
Control signals: determining which operations occur at each stage
Single-cycle design: every instruction completes in one clock cycle
Clock period determined by the slowest instruction
Limitations of single-cycle: wasted time on fast instructions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Trace three different instructions (add, lw, beq) through the single-cycle datapath, highlighting which components are active for each

review.value: The clock period is set by the longest instruction path through the hardware; in a single-cycle design, this is typically the load word instruction which accesses both instruction and data memory
Binary signals that configure multiplexers, enable register writes, and select ALU operations; the control unit generates them by decoding the instruction opcode

learning_objectives: Explain how pipelining improves processor throughput
Identify and resolve data, control, and structural hazards
Understand forwarding, stalling, and branch prediction
readings: Hennessy & Patterson, Computer Architecture, Appendix C

lecture_notes.introduction: Pipelining is the single most important technique in processor design. Like an assembly line in a factory, it overlaps instruction execution so that while one instruction is being decoded, another is being fetched, and another is being executed.
key_concepts: Five-stage pipeline: IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB
Pipeline speedup: ideally N stages gives N times throughput
Data hazards: RAW dependencies between consecutive instructions
Forwarding (bypassing): resolving data hazards without stalling
Control hazards: branches and branch prediction
Structural hazards: resource conflicts
lecture_notes.demonstration: Trace a sequence of 6 instructions through the 5-stage pipeline, identifying hazards and showing how forwarding and stalling resolve them
setup_requirements: Colored pencils for tracking instruction stages

review.value: Instead of waiting for a result to be written to the register file (WB stage), the result is forwarded directly from the EX or MEM stage to the instruction that needs it, eliminating one or two stall cycles
Rather than stalling on every branch, the processor predicts whether the branch will be taken and speculatively executes; modern processors achieve 95%+ accuracy using history-based predictors

learning_objectives: Understand the memory hierarchy and why caches are necessary
Calculate cache hit rates for direct-mapped and set-associative caches
Explain write policies and cache replacement strategies
readings: Patterson & Hennessy, Ch. 5 - Large and Fast: Exploiting Memory Hierarchy
Bryant & O'Hallaron, CS:APP, Ch. 6 - Memory Hierarchy

lecture_notes.introduction: The speed gap between the CPU and main memory is enormous — about 100x. Caches bridge this gap by keeping recently used data close to the processor. Understanding caches explains why array traversal is fast but linked list traversal is slow.
key_concepts: Temporal locality: recently accessed data is likely to be accessed again
Spatial locality: data near recently accessed data is likely to be accessed soon
Direct-mapped, set-associative, and fully-associative caches
Cache address breakdown: tag, index, offset
Write-through vs write-back policies
LRU replacement and cache miss types: compulsory, capacity, conflict
lecture_notes.demonstration: Trace a sequence of memory accesses through a 4-line direct-mapped cache, calculating hit/miss at each step and computing the overall hit rate
setup_requirements: Cache simulator or spreadsheet for calculations

review.value: A 95% cache hit rate with 1-cycle cache and 100-cycle memory gives average access time of 0.95*1 + 0.05*100 = 5.95 cycles; a 90% hit rate gives 10.9 cycles — nearly 2x slower for just 5% more misses
Higher associativity reduces conflict misses but increases hit time and hardware complexity; 4-way or 8-way set-associative caches are the typical sweet spot in modern processors

questions.question: Convert the decimal number -13 to an 8-bit twos complement binary representation. Show your work.
questions.answer: Step 1: 13 in binary = 00001101. Step 2: Flip all bits = 11110010. Step 3: Add 1 = 11110011. So -13 in 8-bit twos complement is 11110011.

questions.question: Write MIPS assembly code that computes the sum of integers from 1 to N, where N is stored in register $s0. Store the result in $s1.
questions.answer:
li   $s1, 0        # sum = 0
    li   $t0, 1        # i = 1
loop:
    bgt  $t0, $s0, done  # if i > N, exit
    add  $s1, $s1, $t0   # sum += i
    addi $t0, $t0, 1     # i++
    j    loop
done:

rubric.key: Correct initialization of sum and counter

questions.question:
Given the following MIPS instruction sequence, identify all data hazards and state whether each can be resolved with forwarding or requires a stall.

add $s0, $t0, $t1
sub $s1, $s0, $t2
lw  $s2, 0($s0)
add $s3, $s2, $s1
questions.answer: Hazard 1: sub reads $s0 written by add — EX-to-EX forwarding resolves this, no stall needed. Hazard 2: lw reads $s0 written by add — EX-to-EX forwarding resolves this, no stall needed. Hazard 3: add reads $s2 written by lw — this is a load-use hazard requiring one stall cycle because the data is not available until the MEM stage. Hazard 4: add reads $s1 written by sub — MEM-to-EX forwarding resolves this, no stall needed.

rubric.key: Correctly distinguishes forwarding from stall cases
Identifies the load-use hazard requiring a stall

questions.question: A direct-mapped cache has 8 cache lines, each holding 4 bytes. Given byte addresses 0, 4, 8, 12, 32, 0, 4, 32, determine the hit/miss for each access.
questions.answer:
Block size = 4 bytes, so block number = address / 4. Cache index = block number % 8.
Addr 0 → block 0 → index 0: MISS (compulsory)
Addr 4 → block 1 → index 1: MISS (compulsory)
Addr 8 → block 2 → index 2: MISS (compulsory)
Addr 12 → block 3 → index 3: MISS (compulsory)
Addr 32 → block 8 → index 0: MISS (conflict, evicts block 0)
Addr 0 → block 0 → index 0: MISS (conflict, evicts block 8)
Addr 4 → block 1 → index 1: HIT
Addr 32 → block 8 → index 0: MISS (conflict, evicts block 0)
Hit rate: 1/8 = 12.5%

rubric.key: Correct block number and index calculations
Correct hit/miss determination for all 8 accesses

major_assignments.title: MIPS Assembly Programming and Processor Simulation
major_assignments.description: Write a set of MIPS assembly programs implementing fundamental algorithms, then simulate their execution through a pipelined processor to analyze hazards and performance. Includes a cache performance analysis component.
objectives: Write correct MIPS assembly for sorting, searching, and string manipulation
Trace instruction execution through a 5-stage pipeline identifying hazards
Analyze cache performance for different access patterns
Connect assembly-level understanding to high-level performance implications
deliverables: MIPS assembly source files for all assigned programs
Pipeline diagrams showing hazard detection and resolution
Cache simulation results and analysis report (2-3 pages)
Reflection document connecting low-level insights to software optimization

criteria: All programs assemble and run correctly in MARS
Programs handle edge cases (empty arrays, single elements)
Register usage follows MIPS calling conventions
Code is commented explaining the purpose of each section
All hazards are correctly identified and classified
Forwarding vs stalling decisions are correct
Pipeline diagrams are complete and accurate
Cycle count analysis is correct
Hit rates calculated correctly for given access patterns
Different cache configurations compared meaningfully
Performance impact quantified with miss penalty calculations
Recommendations for software optimization based on cache behavior
Analysis report is clear and well-organized
Reflection connects low-level concepts to practical software decisions
All diagrams are legible and correctly labeled
Professional writing quality
Correct MIPS assembly programs following calling conventions
Thorough pipeline analysis with accurate hazard resolution
Insightful cache performance analysis with optimization recommendations
Clear documentation connecting low-level understanding to software engineering

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of architecture concepts and calculations

lab_assignments.description: Assembly programming labs and hardware simulation exercises

major_assignments.description: Comprehensive projects integrating multiple architecture topics

midterm_examination.description: Written exam covering ISA, datapath, and pipelining

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all topics including memory hierarchy

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for lab sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in a reduced participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts is encouraged. Assembly code and written analysis must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Copying assembly code or pipeline analyses from any source without attribution constitutes plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need access to the MARS MIPS simulator and a computer capable of running cache simulation tools. Lab computers are available.
